findings.summary: terminal_interface (foundation layer) imports from coding (core layer) at runtime, and logging_monitoring (foundation) conditionally imports from events (extended module), violating the documented upward-only dependency rule.
evidence: terminal_interface/shells/interactive_shell.py:440: from codomyrmex.coding import execute_code — foundation importing from core
logging_monitoring/handlers/event_bridge.py:20-21: from codomyrmex.events.core.event_bus import EventBus — foundation importing from extended service module
CLAUDE.md documents Layer Hierarchy with 'dependencies flow upward only'
findings.suggestion: Move the execute_code call in interactive_shell.py behind an optional import guard with a clear plugin boundary, or accept that terminal_interface is a service-layer module and update the documented layer hierarchy. For event_bridge.py the conditional import is already guarded with try/except, which partially mitigates this, but the logical direction is still downward.

findings.summary: agents/planner modules (core layer) directly import from orchestrator (service layer), and agents/droid/generators imports from documentation (service layer), creating upward imports that the architecture contract prohibits.
evidence: agents/planner/plan_evaluator.py:14: from codomyrmex.orchestrator.workflows.workflow_engine import StepStatus, WorkflowResult
agents/planner/feedback_loop.py:20: from codomyrmex.orchestrator.workflows.workflow_engine import ...
agents/droid/generators/documentation.py:117,125,148,156: imports from codomyrmex.documentation.quality
agents/education.py: from codomyrmex.documentation.education.curriculum import ...
findings.suggestion: Either move shared types like StepStatus and WorkflowResult to a common foundation module (e.g., exceptions or a shared models package), or reclassify agents as a service-layer module. The current documentation should be corrected if the layers are intentionally bidirectional.

findings.summary: Both charts/ and plots/ subdirectories export classes named BarChart, Heatmap, Histogram, LinePlot, PieChart, ScatterPlot, and BoxPlot. The two namespaces use incompatible design patterns (functional+OO wrapper vs pure OO with BasePlot), creating a naming collision visible to consumers.
evidence: charts/bar_chart.py exports BarChart; plots/bar_chart.py also exports BarChart with a different base class
data_visualization/__init__.py imports BarChart from .charts.bar_chart
data_visualization/plots/__init__.py imports BarChart from .bar_chart (plots version)
tests/unit/data_visualization/test_enhanced_methods.py:89 imports BarChart from codomyrmex.data_visualization.plots — the shadow namespace
charts/heatmap.py is 167 lines with full implementation; plots/heatmap.py is 12 lines with a different implementation
findings.suggestion: Pick one design paradigm. Either deprecate charts/ in favor of plots/ (BasePlot hierarchy) or vice versa, and ensure the top-level __init__.py exports from only one source. The current state means codomyrmex.data_visualization.BarChart and codomyrmex.data_visualization.plots.BarChart are different classes.

findings.summary: PipelineStatus, StageStatus, and JobStatus in ci_cd_automation/pipeline/models.py are three independent enum classes with identical member sets (PENDING, RUNNING, SUCCESS, FAILURE, CANCELLED, SKIPPED). A broader scan found TaskStatus repeated in at least 10 different modules with overlapping members.
evidence: ci_cd_automation/pipeline/models.py: PipelineStatus, StageStatus, JobStatus all define identical PENDING/RUNNING/SUCCESS/FAILURE/CANCELLED/SKIPPED values
TaskStatus defined independently in at least 10 modules (agents, collaboration, testing, concurrency, ai_code_editing, generic, agentic_seek, etc.)
No shared base status module or exceptions/models.py that would canonicalize these
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the common execution status values (PENDING, RUNNING, SUCCESS, FAILURE, CANCELLED) into a shared ExecutionStatus enum in the exceptions or a shared models module. Module-specific statuses (DroidStatus, SolverStatus) should remain local, but the generic lifecycle states are cross-cutting.

findings.summary: data_visualization/utils.py:save_plot() swallows all exceptions and returns False instead of raising. All 7 chart creation functions in charts/ call save_plot() without checking the return value, meaning a disk-full or permission error silently produces no file and no indication to the caller.
evidence: utils.py:94-96: except Exception as e: logger.error(...); return False
bar_chart.py:69: save_plot(fig, output_path) — return value discarded
scatter_plot.py:57: save_plot(fig, output_path) — return value discarded
All chart functions return the matplotlib fig object even when save failed, giving callers no way to distinguish successful save from failed save
findings.suggestion: Either raise the exception from save_plot (letting callers handle it), or check the return value in each caller and propagate a failure signal. The CLAUDE.md zero-mock/no-silent-fallbacks policy is directly violated here.

findings.summary: _get_droid_controller() in agents/cli/handlers.py catches all exceptions from create_default_controller() and returns None without re-raising, violating the no-silent-fallbacks policy. The error is logged but callers receive None with no indication of what failed.
evidence: handlers.py:526-531: except Exception as e: logger.exception('Failed to create droid controller'); return None
handle_droid_start() at line 534 calls _get_droid_controller() and checks for None, printing an error, but the root cause exception is not surfaced to users
This pattern is inconsistent with other handler functions in the same file that use logger.exception + print_error + return False
findings.suggestion: Either let _get_droid_controller() propagate the exception to the callers (who already have try/except blocks), or store and re-surface the creation error message so handle_droid_start can show the specific failure reason.

findings.summary: The Plotter class in data_visualization/engines/plotter.py contains 6 methods that each only call kwargs.setdefault() and then forward all arguments to a free function. This is a thin wrapper adding zero policy, translation, or behavior beyond what calling the function directly provides.
evidence: plotter.py:39-42: def bar_chart(self, categories, values, **kwargs): kwargs.setdefault('figure_size', self.figure_size); return create_bar_chart(categories, values, **kwargs)
Same pattern for line_plot, scatter_plot, histogram, pie_chart, heatmap — each method is 3 lines
The only state Plotter holds is figure_size; no caching, no state machine, no policy enforcement
BasePlot in plots/_base.py already provides a richer OO interface with to_html/to_dict/save
findings.suggestion: Either remove the Plotter class and let callers use create_* functions directly (with a module-level DEFAULT_FIGURE_SIZE constant), or have Plotter provide real added value such as consistent theming, output directory management, or batch rendering. The thin wrapper adds surface area without benefit.

findings.summary: Every chart class in charts/ (BarChart, LinePlot, ScatterPlot, Heatmap, etc.) is an OO wrapper whose render() method calls the corresponding create_* function with self attributes unpacked. The classes add no invariants, no validation, and no state beyond what the function already provides. Additionally ScatterPlot.render() does not return the figure while BarChart.render() does, creating an inconsistency.
evidence: bar_chart.py:121-138: BarChart.render() returns create_bar_chart(...) — full delegation
scatter_plot.py:120-131: ScatterPlot.render() calls create_scatter_plot(...) without return — silent drop of return value
line_plot.py:131-150: LinePlot.render() returns create_line_plot(...) — return value preserved
No validation, no caching, no state machine in any of these classes
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to one pattern: either pure functions (simpler, zero cognitive overhead) or proper OO classes that own the matplotlib figure lifecycle, validate inputs at __init__ time, and expose Pythonic APIs. The current state gives the complexity of both without the benefits of either.

findings.summary: numpy>=2.2.0 is a mandatory core dependency in pyproject.toml, but matplotlib (which is the primary consumer of numpy in this codebase) is an optional dependency under [data_visualization]. The engines/plotter.py and charts/heatmap.py import numpy at module level via matplotlib, creating a dependency structure mismatch.
evidence: pyproject.toml:49: numpy>=2.2.0 in [project.dependencies] (mandatory)
pyproject.toml:263-266: data_visualization = [matplotlib>=3.10.0, seaborn>=0.13.0] in [project.optional-dependencies]
plotter.py:8-15: import matplotlib.pyplot and numpy at module level, raises ImportError with install hint if missing
advanced_plotter.py:14-23: imports matplotlib, numpy, pandas, seaborn at module level with ImportError guard
findings.suggestion: Either move numpy to the data_visualization optional group (if its only meaningful use is with matplotlib), or accept that numpy is used across multiple modules and document why it is a mandatory core dependency. The current mismatch is confusing: numpy is forced on all users but its primary consumer (data_visualization) is optional.

findings.summary: pytest.ini sets --cov-fail-under=25 while pyproject.toml coverage.report sets fail_under=31. The actual measured coverage is ~32%. Both gates are far below any meaningful quality threshold and neither gate is enforced consistently — pytest.ini value takes precedence during test runs but pyproject.toml documents a different value.
evidence: pytest.ini:17: --cov-fail-under=25
pyproject.toml:319: fail_under = 31
MEMORY.md references 'Coverage: ~32%' and 'gate 31%'
A 25-31% coverage gate on a project with 124 modules means ~70% of production code paths are untested
findings.suggestion: Align the two coverage configuration values to one consistent threshold. More importantly, set a meaningful ratchet target (e.g., 50-60%) and track it incrementally. The gap between gate (25%) and actual (32%) means the gate provides almost no protection against coverage regressions.

findings.summary: The MCP bridge auto-discovery path (model_context_protocol/discovery/__init__.py), which is the core runtime path that surfaces ~303 tools to PAI, has no dedicated unit tests in the test suite. The recent schema key bug fix (meta.get('schema') vs meta.get('parameters')) suggests this path was not covered.
evidence: MEMORY.md records: 'MCP parameter schema bug FIXED (Mar 3 2026): discovery/__init__.py:275 was reading meta.get(parameters, {}) but decorators.py stores as schema key'
This bug would have been caught by a unit test asserting that @mcp_tool decorated functions expose correct parameter schemas through the discovery path
test_mcp_discovery.py exists but was modified recently, suggesting tests were added after the bug rather than before
findings.suggestion: Add a test that imports a known @mcp_tool decorated function and asserts that the discovery path returns a schema with the correct property names and types. This is a critical path regression test.

findings.summary: At least 416 Python script files contain dead code blocks labeled '# Auto-injected: Load configuration' placed after return statements in main() functions. These blocks import yaml, construct a config path, and print a load message but are unreachable. This is a systemic pattern indicating mass AI-generated code injection went wrong.
evidence: algorithm_orchestrator.py:340: return 0 — then lines 344-352 contain unreachable yaml config loading code labeled '# Auto-injected: Load configuration'
mcp_server_ops.py:188: return 0 — then line 192 starts '# Auto-injected: Load configuration'
grep across scripts/ found 416 instances of '# Auto-injected' comment
The yaml import and config_path construction inside these dead blocks is never executed
findings.suggestion: Run a script to remove all '# Auto-injected' blocks that appear after return statements in main() functions. If config loading is genuinely needed, move it to the correct location before the return statement. The 416 instances suggest this was an automated injection tool that failed to check reachability.

findings.summary: The repository root contains 14 ad-hoc test scripts (test_image.py, test_gen.py, test_keys.py, test_models.py, test_veo_*.py, fail.py, etc.) that are not part of the pytest test suite, use no test framework, and appear to be one-off exploration scripts left in the root.
evidence: test_image.py: script-level code with no test functions, uses Google genai directly at module level
fail.py: intentionally raises ValueError — a debugging artifact
test_gen.py, test_keys.py, test_models.py: ad-hoc exploration scripts with no assertions
pytest.ini testpaths = src/codomyrmex so these are excluded from the test run, but their presence suggests accumulated development debris
findings.suggestion: Move any scripts worth keeping to scripts/examples/ or a dedicated exploration/ directory. Delete the pure debugging artifacts (fail.py, test scripts that just print outputs). These files at the root level create confusion about what is production code vs experimental scripts.

findings.summary: 16 Python files including 14 test_*.py scripts and fail.py exist at the repository root, outside the src/ layout and excluded from the test runner. This breaks the 'src layout' packaging pattern and pollutes the root with development artifacts.
evidence: ls / shows 16 .py files in repository root
pytest.ini testpaths = src/codomyrmex — root scripts are excluded from test collection
fail.py is a single-function script that raises ValueError intentionally — debugging artifact
fix_docstrings_v2.py is a maintenance utility — belongs in scripts/ or tools/
findings.suggestion: Move fix_docstrings_v2.py to scripts/tools/. Move any test scripts that are genuine examples to scripts/examples/. Delete pure debugging artifacts (fail.py). Consider adding root-level *.py to .gitignore to prevent future accumulation.

findings.summary: data_visualization contains two parallel subdirectories (charts/ and plots/) that provide overlapping chart types with different API styles. Neither is deprecated nor clearly preferred. The top-level __init__.py sources from charts/ for most types but tests access plots/ directly, creating an undocumented split.
evidence: charts/ contains: area_chart, bar_chart, box_plot, heatmap, histogram, line_plot, pie_chart, scatter_plot
plots/ contains: area, bar_chart, box, heatmap, histogram, line_plot, pie, scatter (plus 10 additional types)
data_visualization/__init__.py imports from .charts for the core types
data_visualization/plots/__init__.py exports a different BarChart class with the same name
findings.suggestion: Consolidate chart implementations into one directory. The plots/ directory has a richer set of specialized chart types (candlestick, gantt, sankey, etc.) that charts/ lacks. A clean approach would be to keep plots/ as the canonical location and have charts/ be a compatibility shim or be removed entirely.

findings.summary: The data_visualization module has three independent base classes (BasePlot, BaseComponent, BaseReport) that share identical method signatures (render, to_dict, save, __str__, __repr__) but share no common ancestor. This forces repeated protocol implementation and prevents polymorphic usage across the three hierarchies.
evidence: BasePlot, BaseComponent, BaseReport each define render()->str, to_dict()->dict, __str__, __repr__
BaseComponent omits save() while BasePlot and BaseReport both define it with different signatures
No shared Renderable or HTMLElement protocol/ABC that would unify these
Three separate inheritance chains with no shared mixin
findings.suggestion: Extract a Renderable protocol or ABC with render()->str and to_dict()->dict as the shared interface. Have BasePlot, BaseComponent, and BaseReport all implement it. This would allow generic dashboard code to accept any Renderable without isinstance checks.

findings.summary: create_heatmap is defined in both data_visualization/charts/heatmap.py (canonical, 109 lines) and data_visualization/engines/plotter.py (copy, 73 lines). The plotter.py version is decorated with @monitor_performance but otherwise duplicates the logic from charts/heatmap.py without calling it.
evidence: charts/heatmap.py:22: def create_heatmap(...) — 109 lines, full implementation with theme parameter
engines/plotter.py:71: def create_heatmap(...) — 73 lines, @monitor_performance decorator, missing theme parameter
plotter.py imports from charts.bar_chart, charts.histogram, charts.line_plot, charts.pie_chart, charts.scatter_plot but NOT from charts.heatmap
The __init__.py exports create_heatmap from charts.heatmap, but Plotter.heatmap() calls the local plotter.py version
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate create_heatmap from engines/plotter.py and have Plotter.heatmap() import from charts.heatmap instead. If performance monitoring is needed, apply @monitor_performance to the charts version, or wrap it in plotter.py with a one-line call.

findings.summary: data_visualization/engines/advanced_plotter.py executes plt.style.use('default'), sns.set_palette('husl'), and warnings.filterwarnings('ignore') at module import time. These are global mutations of matplotlib and warnings state that affect all subsequent use of these libraries in the same process.
evidence: advanced_plotter.py:37: plt.style.use('default') — at module scope, not inside any function
advanced_plotter.py:38: sns.set_palette('husl') — global seaborn state mutation
advanced_plotter.py:39: warnings.filterwarnings('ignore', category=UserWarning) — suppresses all UserWarnings globally
The file is 1005 lines and is flagged in MEMORY.md as a known oversized file
findings.suggestion: Move these global state mutations into a setup function or a context manager that callers opt into. At minimum, scope warnings.filterwarnings to specific warning messages rather than all UserWarnings. The global palette/style mutation will affect any test or caller that runs after importing this module.

findings.summary: Four chart files (bar_chart.py, scatter_plot.py, histogram.py, pie_chart.py) in data_visualization/charts/ import stdlib 'import logging' in addition to from codomyrmex.logging_monitoring import get_logger. The stdlib logging import is only used in the __main__ guard to check if root logger has handlers. This creates a dual-logging dependency.
evidence: bar_chart.py:8: import logging — used only in if __name__ == '__main__' block for logging.basicConfig
scatter_plot.py:8: import logging — same pattern
histogram.py:8: import logging — same pattern
pie_chart.py:8: import logging — same pattern
line_plot.py does NOT have this import (it uses os module instead) — inconsistency within sibling files
findings.suggestion: Remove the 'import logging' from the module-level imports and move it inside the if __name__ == '__main__' guard where it is actually used, or remove the __main__ blocks entirely (they are development artifacts not needed in a library). This keeps the module's production import path clean.

findings.summary: The data_visualization module exposes three distinct API styles simultaneously: (1) free functions create_bar_chart(categories, values), (2) charts/ OO wrapper classes (BarChart wrapping the function), (3) plots/ BasePlot subclasses with different constructor signatures and to_html() behavior. All three are exported from different namespaces with no guidance on which to use.
evidence: __init__.py exports: create_bar_chart (free function), BarChart (charts/ class with .render()), generate_report (report classes)
plots/__init__.py exports: BarChart (different class with BasePlot.to_html())
engines/plotter.py exports: Plotter class with .bar_chart() method
mcp_tools.py:28-35 calls dv.create_bar_chart (free function path) for the public tool API
Each style has different constructor signatures, return types, and rendering behaviors
findings.suggestion: Choose one primary API surface and document the others as deprecated or internal. The MCP tools already use the free function path; making that the canonical external API and marking the class wrappers as internal implementation details would simplify the design. The BasePlot hierarchy in plots/ is the most extensible pattern for new chart types.

findings.summary: ScatterPlot.render() in charts/scatter_plot.py does not return the figure from create_scatter_plot(), while every other chart class in the same module family (BarChart, LinePlot, Heatmap) does return the figure. This is a behavioral inconsistency in sibling classes.
evidence: scatter_plot.py:120-131: render() calls create_scatter_plot() without return — returns None implicitly
bar_chart.py:128-138: render() uses 'return create_bar_chart(...)' — returns Figure or None
line_plot.py:139-150: render() uses 'return create_line_plot(...)' — returns Figure or None
heatmap.py (charts/) render() also returns the create_heatmap() result
findings.suggestion: Add 'return' to ScatterPlot.render() at line 120: 'return create_scatter_plot(...)'. This is a one-character fix that restores behavioral consistency. Consider adding a test that asserts all chart class .render() methods return a matplotlib Figure when given valid data.
